messages.author: Zorphix
messages.text: The center wire of the coax goes to the one pointing up, the shield (ground) goes to the legs pointing down. Most people just use a connector to build the antenna onto so that causes the mount and the legs pointing down to be grounded automatically. The nice thing with 869mhz is the higher in frequency you go the smaller/shorter the antenna becomes... another benefit for 900mhz vs 433mhz
messages.content_html: The center wire of the coax goes to the one pointing up, the shield (ground) goes to the legs pointing down. Most people just use a connector to build the antenna onto so that causes the mount and the legs pointing down to be grounded automatically. The nice thing with 869mhz is the higher in frequency you go the smaller/shorter the antenna becomes... another benefit for 900mhz vs 433mhz

messages.author: TonyA1960
messages.text: Hi Guys, PE also has 433 MC. 433.750 Originally started with MT, had about 50 nodes running, mainly members of the ham club, but quite a few "preppers" Now the serious users, ham club, have moved to MC. messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: V4 workes great. Running one on Meshcore in Cape Town now. 868MHz. You can use the "Nightly Build" to get the firmware to run higher power and then just use the correct dBm in the app to get a radiated power of 500mA after considering loses due to cable, antenna and positioning. (edited)

(edited)

messages.author: TonyA1960
messages.text: PE and Garden route are on 433.750
messages.content_html: PE and Garden route are on 433.750

messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: 868MHz (UK/EU/SA) and 915MHz (US) are the most common frequencies used on Meshcore and Meshtastic at present.
messages.content_html: 868MHz (UK/EU/SA) and 915MHz (US) are the most common frequencies used on Meshcore and Meshtastic at present.

messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: Problem 433 MHz faces in South Afrca is that power outputs are limited to 10dBm which is 10mW (unless you have a valid HAM license and then you can only send unencrypted traffic) while in the 868MHz band you are allowed up to 27dBm which is 500mW (1% duty cycle). Even though theoretically the 433MHz band transmits further in obstructed terrain for the same power output the difference between 10mW and 500mW is so great that 868MHz exceeds 433Mhz range.
messages.content_html: Problem 433 MHz faces in South Afrca is that power outputs are limited to 10dBm which is 10mW (unless you have a valid HAM license and then you can only send unencrypted traffic) while in the 868MHz band you are allowed up to 27dBm which is 500mW (1% duty cycle). Even though theoretically the 433MHz band transmits further in obstructed terrain for the same power output the difference between 10mW and 500mW is so great that 868MHz exceeds 433Mhz range.

messages.author: TonyA1960
messages.text: We are achieving 100km plus with 433. Also one of our primary use cases is in remote bushy areas. 433 penetration is far better. Well placed repeaters are key to a good functioning mesh.
messages.content_html: We are achieving 100km plus with 433. Also one of our primary use cases is in remote bushy areas. 433 penetration is far better. Well placed repeaters are key to a good functioning mesh.

messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: Yeah, I don't disagree with you. 433MHz is great. I have some 433MHz nodes as well. I just think 868Mhz offers more robust connections for those that don't have a HAM license.
messages.content_html: Yeah, I don't disagree with you. 433MHz is great. I have some 433MHz nodes as well. I just think 868Mhz offers more robust connections for those that don't have a HAM license.

messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: Unlike client nodes on MT companion nodes on MC don't relay messages. MC relies on routers for that.
messages.content_html: Unlike client nodes on MT companion nodes on MC don't relay messages. MC relies on routers for that.

messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: Keeps mesh traffic lower. MC floods to find the best path between companion nodes and then sets that as the route. Only the routers on that best path relay messages which saves on airtime. If the path fails after a 3rd retry it floods to find a new best path. MC doesn't automatically transmit telemetry. This also saves airtime. MC has a hop limit of 64 if I am remembering correctly. It has less issues with network congestion for the above reasons. There are so many client nodes on MT in Cape Town (in particular) that the mesh is heavily congested and nodes even slightly further away from each other have unreliable connections...unless using MQTT but then MQTT defeats the object of having off grid comms. (edited)
messages.content_html: Keeps mesh traffic lower. MC floods to find the best path between companion nodes and then sets that as the route. Only the routers on that best path relay messages which saves on airtime. If the path fails after a 3rd retry it floods to find a new best path. MC doesn't automatically transmit telemetry. This also saves airtime. MC has a hop limit of 64 if I am remembering correctly. It has less issues with network congestion for the above reasons. There are so many client nodes on MT in Cape Town (in particular) that the mesh is heavily congested and nodes even slightly further away from each other have unreliable connections...unless using MQTT but then MQTT defeats the object of having off grid comms. (edited)

messages.text: @Delta1 from where I am, I am unable to see Flamingo repeater. I tried to discover repeaters, send messages and send adverts (both zero hop and flood routed) but nothing seems to happen. I really am in a bad spot, with too many obstacles. My Meshtastic roof node (which is now not up the roof due to maintenance) used to traceroute with MT Flamingo node, so I guess that if I could put the MC node up the roof it would be a bit better...?

messages.author: Delta1
messages.text: Without MQTT, I cannot directly RF receive from many Cape Town nodes on MT. I see them firing off messages on the chat link, on I think your website but without MQTT I don't see many of them. At best, I can see some of the nodes pop up in the nodes list but receiving messages from them is near impossible. The MT flood protocol in conjunction with the huge number of Client nodes in the network sees the hop limits consumed before the messages cross the network in full. (edited)
messages.content_html: Without MQTT, I cannot directly RF receive from many Cape Town nodes on MT. I see them firing off messages on the chat link, on I think your website but without MQTT I don't see many of them. At best, I can see some of the nodes pop up in the nodes list but receiving messages from them is near impossible. The MT flood protocol in conjunction with the huge number of Client nodes in the network sees the hop limits consumed before the messages cross the network in full. (edited)
